sentence: The appellants who are admittedly displaced persons from West Pakistan were granted quasi permanent allotment of 24 standard acres and 15 3/4 units in the village of Raikot in Ludhiana District in 1949.

The two villages are, however, 25 miles or so distant from each other.
During the pendency of this application he died and after his death, the, application was continued by the appellants.
This application was rejected by the Assistant Custodian on July 23, 1951 on the ground that no land was available in the villageRaikot.
These 14 allottees preferred an application for review of the order cancelling their allotment on the grounds that they were not entitled to allotment of suburban lands at all.
The Judgment of the Court was delivered by MUDHOLKAR J.

The Punjab and Haryana High Court has ruled that the Deputy Custodian General, Evacuee Property, Now Delhi should not have cancelled the order of allotment of land in the village Raikot made in favour of the appellants in the year 1949.

The case involves an appeal by displaced persons from West Pakistan, who were granted quasi-permanent allotment of land in Raikot village in 1949, against the cancellation of their allotment and subsequent allotment of land in Karodian village.

Their father, who was entitled to a larger allotment in another village, had applied for consolidation of lands with his sons' allotment in Raikot, but this application was rejected due to lack of available land.
The cancellation of the allotment in Raikot and subsequent allotment in Karodian was made by the Additional Custodian for the State of Punjab in 1952, citing Rule 14(6) of the Evacuee Property Rules.
The appellants challenged this decision, arguing that their jurisdiction to revise the order had not been taken away by the provisions of the Rehabilitation Act of 1954.
However, the Deputy Custodian General dismissed their application, citing the Rehabilitation Act's provisions as taking away his jurisdiction to deal with matters of this kind.

The appellants appealed to the Supreme Court of India, arguing that there was no specific provision in the Act that prevented them from dealing with the allotment or cancellation of evacuee property after its commencement.
The decision of the Delhi High Court in Bal Mukund case is not applicable to the present case as it was based on the provisions of the 1954 Act.
The , under the , has no power to deal with evacuee property acquired under the .
The decision of the Delhi High Court in Bal Mukund case is not applicable
